agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a cross-bounded-context integration that fundamentally changes the autonomy of the Sales context and introduces a hard coupling between two previously independent domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request threatens to collapse two distinct bounded contexts into a single coupled system.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) in the Warehouse context representing the current quantity of a Product on hand. Owned exclusively by Context A."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A sales aggregate in Context B representing a customer's intent to purchase one or more products."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order, referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An entity that exists in both contexts but with different meanings — in Warehouse it is an item with a StockLevel; in Sales it is a catalog reference with pricing and availability semantics."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A domain concept currently MISSING — a time-bounded claim on stock that bridges the two contexts without transferring ownership of the StockLevel concept into Sales."},{"term":"Availability Indicator","definition":"A eventually-consistent projection of stock sufficiency, published by Context A and consumed (read-only) by Context B for display purposes. NOT authoritative for order blocking."},{"term":"Order Blocking","definition":"The proposed act of rejecting or holding an Order when stock is deemed insufficient. This is a new domain event that does not currently exist in either context."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel is owned exclusively by Context A (Warehouse). No other context may directly read or mutate it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If Sales directly queries or modifies StockLevel, we lose data ownership, introduce race conditions, and corrupt the Warehouse aggregate's consistency boundary.","testable_assertion":"No code in Context B directly accesses or writes to Context A's StockLevel storage."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"StockLevel cannot go below 0.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A negative StockLevel represents a physically impossible state — you cannot pick items that do not exist in the warehouse.","testable_assertion":"Given a Product with StockLevel = 0, when a pick is attempted, then the operation is rejected and StockLevel remains 0."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"An Order placement must not require synchronous availability of the Warehouse context.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If Sales cannot accept orders when Warehouse is down or slow, a single infrastructure failure in one context causes total business stoppage. The original design explicitly decoupled these contexts for this reason.","testable_assertion":"Given the Warehouse service is unreachable, when a customer places an Order, then the Order is still accepted (possibly in a provisional state) rather than failing entirely."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Stock sufficiency checks for order blocking must use a reservation mechanism, not a point-in-time query.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"A point-in-time stock check is a classic race condition — two orders could both see stock=1 and both proceed. This is a strong design constraint but could be relaxed to a simpler model if the business accepts occasional overselling.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X has StockLevel = 1 and two concurrent Orders each request Quantity = 1, then at most one Order is confirmed; the other is blocked or waitlisted."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to Sales is eventually consistent, not real-time, and must never be used as the authoritative source for order blocking decisions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Real-time stock visibility across bounded contexts is a distributed systems impossibility without merging the contexts. Using a stale indicator to block orders would cause both false blocks (lost revenue) and false approvals (overselling). The display indicator and the blocking decision must use different mechanisms.","testable_assertion":"Given the availability indicator shows 'in stock' for Product X, when a customer places an Order, the blocking decision is made via the reservation mechanism, not the cached indicator."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A blocked Order must transition to a defined state (e.g., 'Pending Stock' or 'Rejected'), not silently disappear.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The customer placed an order — the business has an obligation to communicate outcome. A silently dropped order is data loss and a customer trust violation.","testable_assertion":"Given an Order is blocked due to insufficient stock, then the Order exists in state 'PendingStock' or 'Rejected' and the customer is notified."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Stock reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL) and expire if the order is not confirmed within the window.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Without TTL, abandoned or failed orders permanently reduce available stock — a business policy concern, not a physical invariant. The TTL duration is a tunable business parameter.","testable_assertion":"Given a stock reservation is created for an Order, when the TTL expires without Order confirmation, then the reserved stock is released back to available StockLevel."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an Order with OrderLines referencing Product X (Quantity = 5)","when":"Context B requests a stock reservation from Context A","then":"Context A either confirms the reservation (reducing available stock by 5) or rejects it (stock insufficient), and Context B transitions the Order to 'Confirmed' or 'PendingStock' accordingly"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"The Warehouse context publishes a stock-level-changed event for Product X","when":"Context B receives the event","then":"The 'stock available' indicator for Product X is updated in the Sales UI (eventually consistent, acceptable lag defined by SLA)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The Warehouse context is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer places an Order","then":"The Order is accepted in a provisional state and the reservation is retried when Context A recovers; the customer is NOT shown an error"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Two concurrent Orders each request the last unit of Product X","when":"Both reservation requests reach Context A","then":"Exactly one reservation succeeds and one fails; no overselling occurs and StockLevel never goes below 0"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A stock reservation exists for an Order that is never confirmed","when":"The reservation TTL expires","then":"The reserved quantity is released and becomes available for other orders"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An Order is blocked due to insufficient stock","when":"Stock is later replenished","then":"The system can optionally re-evaluate pending orders (business policy decision, not an invariant)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Partial stock availability — Order has 3 lines, stock is sufficient for 2 but not the 3rd","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: block the entire order, allow partial fulfillment, or split the order. This is an unresolved business policy that MUST be defined before implementation."},{"scenario":"Stock reserved but warehouse discovers damaged goods during picking","expected_behavior":"Reservation was valid at time of creation but physical stock is now lower. The warehouse context must emit a reservation-failed event, and the Order must transition to a compensating state."},{"scenario":"High-frequency stock changes (flash sale) cause event flood to Sales context","expected_behavior":"The availability indicator should be throttled/debounced. Display lag is acceptable; incorrect blocking decisions are not."},{"scenario":"Customer places order, reservation succeeds, but payment fails","expected_behavior":"The reservation must be explicitly released. If not, stock is locked until TTL — potentially blocking other customers during the wait."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Sales catalog but has never been stocked in Warehouse","expected_behavior":"Context A has no StockLevel record for this Product. The reservation request should be rejected with a clear 'product unknown to warehouse' distinction, not confused with 'out of stock'."},{"scenario":"Bulk/B2B order requesting quantity larger than total warehouse capacity","expected_behavior":"Should be handled differently from standard consumer orders — potentially routed to a manual approval flow rather than auto-blocked."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on stock prevents overselling (reduces costly backorders, cancellations, and customer compensation). However, false blocks from stale data or race conditions directly lose revenue. The reservation mechanism adds infrastructure cost but pays for itself in reduced fulfillment failures.","operational":"Introduces operational coupling between two previously independent teams/systems. Warehouse outages now impact Sales (mitigated by provisional order acceptance). Adds monitoring burden: reservation TTLs, event lag SLAs, and stock synchronization health all become operational concerns.","user":"Customers gain transparency (stock indicator) and faster feedback on order feasibility. However, if implemented poorly, customers see confusing states ('PendingStock'), false out-of-stock indicators, or orders silently stuck. The UX for blocked orders must be carefully designed.","risk":"HIGH — This is a cross-bounded-context integration touching the most critical business flow (order placement). Incorrect implementation causes either lost revenue (false blocks) or fulfillment failures (overselling). The 'real-time' requirement in the spec is physically impossible in a distributed system and must be reframed as 'eventually consistent with defined SLA'.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain stock visibility and faster order feedback, but risk confusion from new order states (PendingStock/Rejected) and potential false out-of-stock signals"},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock indicator in their UI, reducing manual stock checks. Must be trained on new order states and understand the indicator is not perfectly real-time."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Now has downstream consumers of their stock data. Changes to stock management processes, inventory adjustments, or system maintenance now have cross-context impact."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform Team","impact":"Must build and maintain the integration infrastructure: event bus, reservation service, TTL management, monitoring. Ongoing operational burden."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Order states now include 'PendingStock' — revenue recognition rules may need updating. Reservation expiry creates a new class of order lifecycle events to account for."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is underspecified in critical areas: it says 'automatically block' but does not define partial availability handling, does not acknowledge the distributed systems reality (says 'real time' which is impossible across bounded contexts), and does not specify the blocking mechanism. The phrase 'without checking Context A' in the existing design explicitly established decoupling — the new feature directly contradicts this without acknowledging the architectural implications. Multiple ambiguities remain unresolved.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel ownership must remain in Context A","Synchronous coupling between contexts is architecturally wrong","A reservation pattern is the correct integration mechanism","The availability indicator must be eventually consistent, not real-time"],"low_confidence_areas":["Partial availability policy (block whole order vs. partial fulfillment)","Reservation TTL duration","Whether blocked orders should auto-retry on restock","How the 'stock available' indicator SLA should be defined","Whether B2B/bulk orders follow the same blocking rules"],"assumptions":["The business intends to prevent overselling, not merely warn about it","Both contexts are separately deployable services (not a monolith)","An event-based integration mechanism is available or can be built","The business accepts eventually-consistent stock display in exchange for system resilience"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'automatically block it if stock is insufficient' but does not define behavior for partial availability — when some OrderLines can be fulfilled but others cannot. This is a critical business policy decision that changes the entire order lifecycle.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team","Finance"],"options":["A: Block the entire order if any line is insufficient","B: Allow partial fulfillment and split the order","C: Allow partial fulfillment and backorder the rest"],"recommendation":"Option A is simplest and safest for initial implementation. Partial fulfillment (B/C) is a significant domain concept that should be a separate feature."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec requests 'real-time' stock availability in the Sales UI. Real-time consistency across bounded contexts is a distributed systems impossibility without merging the contexts. The acceptable staleness SLA must be defined.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Sub-second via direct synchronous call to Warehouse (creates hard coupling)","B: Event-driven with <5 second typical lag (preserves autonomy)","C: Polling-based with 30-60 second refresh (simplest)"],"recommendation":"Option B — event-driven with a defined SLA (e.g., 95th percentile < 5 seconds). Preserves bounded context autonomy while meeting practical business needs."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The existing design explicitly states 'Orders are placed by customers without checking Context A.' The new feature requests that orders BE checked against Context A and blocked. This is a fundamental contradiction in the domain model — the Sales context was intentionally autonomous, and this feature removes that autonomy. The spec must acknowledge this is an architectural change, not just a feature addition.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Sales Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Redefine Context B to have a dependency on Context A (breaking the original decoupling)","B: Introduce an anti-corruption layer / integration context that mediates between A and B without either owning the other","C: Keep order acceptance autonomous but add a post-acceptance validation step"],"recommendation":"Option B or C — introduce a mediating mechanism (saga, process manager, or integration context) that coordinates without collapsing the bounded contexts. Option C is most faithful to the existing architecture."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The feature as specified ('real-time stock indicator') implies synchronous cross-context reads, which would collapse bounded context boundaries.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (StockLevel ownership), BR-3 (Sales must not depend on Warehouse availability)"},{"violation":"The feature as specified ('automatically block') without defining a reservation mechanism will cause race conditions under concurrent orders.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 (reservation mechanism required), BR-2 (StockLevel cannot go below 0)"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a cross-bounded-context integration that fundamentally changes the autonomy of the Sales context and introduces a hard coupling between two previously independent domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Orde
